title: One Host, Many Servers
concept: A single host application connecting to multiple independent MCP servers simultaneously, each a separate capability.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about the MCP Server. What is its main job?

options.text: A massive cloud database that stores all LLM weights.
A lightweight program exposing specific tools or data sources.
The main user interface where you type chat messages.

exercises.explain: An MCP server is a separate, lightweight program that exposes specific capabilities (like tools or resources) to a host application.

cards.title: Supercharging Your AI Host
cards.body: We know an MCP server can expose a tool, but an AI agent isn't very smart if it can only do one thing. What if it could read your local files, check your calendar, and search the web all at the same time? Let's see how hosts coordinate this!

cards.title: Multiplexing Connections
cards.body: A single MCP Host can establish separate connection channels to multiple independent MCP Servers at the same time. This lets the AI combine specialized tools, like a GitHub helper and a local database explorer, into a single workspace.

cards.title: The Smartphone Dashboard
cards.body: Imagine your AI host as a smartphone. Instead of building a camera, a GPS map, and a music player directly into the phone's operating system, the phone connects to separate, dedicated apps. Each app does one thing perfectly, and the phone talks to all of them at once.

cards.title: A Multi-Tool AI Assistant
cards.body: Claude Desktop runs as a host. It connects to a 'Weather Server' to fetch local forecasts, and simultaneously connects to a 'Postgres Server' to run database queries, giving the LLM two distinct super-powers at once.

cards.title: The Myth of the 'Do-It-All' Server
cards.body: You might think you need to bundle all your tools into one giant, messy server program. In reality, keeping servers small and separate makes them safer to run, easier to debug, and simple to mix-and-match.

cards.title: Parallel Connections
cards.body: Instead of a single giant server, the host connects to multiple clean, separated pipes.

left.text: Reads and writes local code files.

right.text: Fetches live weather or web search data.

exercises.prompt: How does a single Host application manage connections to multiple MCP servers?

options.text: The servers talk directly to each other without the host knowing.
The host connects to each server individually and routes requests to them.
The host merges all server code into a single file before running it.
Each server must run on a different physical computer.

exercises.explain: The Host acts as the central hub, routing user prompts to the LLM and delegating specific data or action requests to the appropriate specialized MCP servers.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An MCP Host is strictly limited to connecting to only one MCP Server at a time.
exercises.explain: An MCP host can connect to as many servers as your computer's resources allow, combining many different capabilities.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe why running separate MCP servers is safe:
exercises.sentence: Running tools in isolated processes prevents one broken server from crashing the entire host application.
exercises.explain: Because MCP servers run as isolated processes, a bug or crash in one server won't bring down the host or affect other connected tools.

exercises.prompt: Identify the response that looks like an AI hallucinating an impossible merged MCP protocol command rather than using distinct servers:

options.text: Host: 'Calling Git Server to fetch commits...' followed by 'Calling Slack Server to post updates...'
Host: 'Initiating hybrid Git-Slack multi-channel protocol merge command to broadcast live commits...'
Host: 'Querying Database Server for user list. Querying Email Server to send newsletters.'

exercises.explain: Option B represents a typical LLM generation pretending to be a tool output where it hallucinates mixing two distinct server APIs into a single non-existent protocol command, rather than keeping them clean and separate.

exercises.prompt: Your AI assistant (Host) is connected to two MCP servers: a Database Tool server and a File System tool server. If the Database server crashes due to a network timeout, what happens to the File System tool?

options.text: The host app will crash completely because all servers share a single process.
Only the database tool fails; the file system tool remains active and usable.
The host automatically merges both servers into one temporary program.
The host stops accepting any tool inputs until the database server is rebooted.

exercises.explain: An MCP host manages multiple independent server connections simultaneously. If one server (like a weather API) goes offline, the other servers (like local file access or database tools) continue to work perfectly because they run in isolated processes.
